Hey, this is Sean and Andre. It is week three for the Momentum Builder Workshop Lean Edition. This is the first time we're going to experiment with a sort of free-form audio. I'm just going to talk about this week's content. I'll let Andre explain his vision for this. This was his idea. Oh, well, I've been thrown into that. So, both Sean and I are visual thinkers, and by the end of the course, I think everyone's going to be visual thinkers because we're going to force be it to everybody. There's going to be visuals everywhere. So, this is a mental visual, which is this lesson we've... We're thinking of it as a painting. We've presented this 1,500-word piece of text. There's a lot of precision in the writing. There's no fluff. But what's lacking is a bit of the nuances. Then that's what this audio is about. We're going to try and add some color to the painting that we've already given you. I think that's the big idea here. I'm going to see... I mean, you guys are going to let us know with your feedback if this format is enjoyable. It's not going to be perfect. We've promised ourselves that we're going to create imperfect work in this workshop. That's my story, and I was thinking to it. That's the way we set the standard. Nothing you're about to hear is going to be perfect. We're hoping it will be good. So, of course, we've had this conversation. We've started the conversation several times today, and we've actually stumbled onto some things that are really interesting. So rather than forget that we haven't shared those with everyone, I think what I'll do is I'll just describe the conversation we just had twice. Then we can take off from there. So, as we were talking about what we wanted to accomplish on today's call, just to give ourselves a little bit of structure to get us started, what we realized is that the Momentum Builder workshop, this version of it, this content that we're focusing on, the sprint from ad through six emails, the entire process is really at the macro level, and the micro level is about beginning with the end of mind. So, when we think about the thematic arc, we're starting where we know we're taking the ideal prospect. We know we want them to be a happy customer, and we're working backward from that end. We're asking ourselves at every stage at this macro level, like what's required to make that happen, and we're building out a system that does that. So, then within that, we're also repeating that same process. If you think about week one in the Facebook ad, we know that we want the right prospect to take the next step at the end of the Facebook ad and click through the landing page. So, the entire landing page is engineered, I'm sorry, the entire Facebook ad is engineered from that end point to create something that will get the right people to click. So, today we're talking about the landing page, and the landing page, we've now up the stakes. We know that we want the right person to give their primary email address at the end to enter our world and give us permission to communicate directly with them. So, we're beginning from that perspective, and then working backward to what we need to create to make that happen, instead of thinking about, okay, what's our headline going to be, or what should our first hub subhead do? So, we're not trying to force them step by step forward, we're trying to pull them toward the destination that we know. We know where we're taking them, and that's true for the entire thematic arc, and it's true for the landing page. We know we're pulling them toward something, and once we know what we're pulling them toward, we can then go back to the beginning and decide what the ingredients are that are necessary to pull the right person toward that goal. So, that's my entire contribution to the conversation today. I'm very taken away. Right, so, you know, just to reiterate elements of that, there's an image that's come with this lesson, and I think if you're a visual person, you want to cast your eyes at the bottom of the image, and that's our starting point. It's the idea of, you know, getting the right people to respond to the call to action, which is them to get onto an email list, and there's a few dynamics at this point, is we don't just want, as Sean said, anybody to give us the email address, we also don't just want any email address, and we also don't want people to feel coerced, nothing that we write or should write is anything to do with being, you know, forcing or pushing somebody, but there's a very different posture when someone is like, they can't wait to do a certain action, you know, and I think we've all experienced that, I mean, I've experienced that many times where I'm so giddy with excitement to order something, and maybe there's an opt-in form in front of the order thing, or maybe it's just a direct order, and I'm excited every step of the way, because something's happened, you know. So that's the whole idea over here, we want to create this dynamic of somebody that can't wait to, so when the offer is made, you know, we need your name and email address, and there's friction at that point, because you'll see when you read the text, or you should have read it already, that, you know, we're making it difficult in a sense, not super easy, so we're not removing friction. Whenever we can, we want to add layers of friction, it doesn't have to be big, huge things, it's just ideas, where's an idea that creates friction could be, you know, maybe this isn't for you, that whole idea, right? So, and then they're thinking, what? Of course it's for me, and obviously some context can be wrapped around that, but that's the sort of idea and the posture we want somebody to have as they go through this, and I think one of the things that we shared in the lesson-lesson is that you can create, well, we recommend, we highly suggest that you do the exercise on creating a customer POV, and doing that exercise of inserting yourself into the customer's mind, and then as you, you know, either through audio or written, you know, Sean and I love to write because we think on the page, and as we're writing, ideas are forming. I'm not that great by doing that, when I'm just talking like I'm doing now and stumbling over my words, where Sean's a lot better at that, but, you know, just whatever's easiest, just do that version. And then just riff your way through your landing page, obviously, once it's created, and insert yourself into the customer's point of view, just like our example, but yours will be more detailed because you'll actually have the elements on your page. And so, the whole idea is to, by the time somebody gets to the call to action at the bottom, which is the whole idea over there is, what does somebody need to know or believe to want to open the door, you know, open the opt-in door and go through and get to the other side to see what else is behind the door. So that's the whole frame that we want to get to, and then we do that by having a hook, which is then, now we go to the top of the page, because now that question that we have for the call to action informs what our hook could be. And then what our thesis statement could be, whether that's implicit or explicit, and then the whole body copy then just becomes, you know, where you develop, where you explain, where you support your arguments up at the top. So in the upside down triangle at the top is where you do that, is where you have your hook and your thesis statement, and that's where you set the stage, you put your stake in the ground, this is my claim, this is my argument, and the rest in the rectangle block is where you prove that, support that, demonstrate that, give evidence, and then the triangle at the bottom is where you recap your thesis statement, your big idea, your big argument, and you have the call to action. So I think that's the super big, the super 30,000 foot view. And I think the burning question, I use that phrase, I'm staring at the text on the screen, so I'm repeating something we already wrote, I wrote it accidentally, but this question, if we did the same exercise ourselves right now, and imagine that we're taking the point of view of our students who are going through this workshop, I think one of the things, if after I read this entire module, I would now be thinking about sitting down, staring at a screen, or maybe I have had a paper and a pencil or a pen, and I'm wondering to myself, okay, great, now what the hell do I actually do? It's time to write, what am I supposed to do? And from that perspective, I think it's called out in the text, but I want to draw everyone's attention to this one part, which is, when you think about the organizing question, what does someone need to know, believe, internalize, or accept before she desperately wants to step through the door we've created with our call to action when it's time to opt in. Those words were chosen very, very carefully and very precisely, because that gives you the broadest question to answer when you sit down to write. And at first, it might just be a collection of bullets, it might just be, if you read our email we sent out several weeks or a month or so ago, and you're writing processes or that collection of beats, this could be a place to use those beats, like just ask yourself, what does somebody need to know? I mean, just make your list, let me know these three or four things, okay, what do they actually have to believe about this? Like, well, they might have to believe that you're credible, they might have to believe they can do it under the right circumstance, whatever it is, just make your list, and you're making your ingredients. Now, when we get to internalize or accept, now we're upping the stakes. And if you look at the words that we chose, no believe, internalize, accept, that's actually a progression. And you can know something, not necessarily believe it. You can believe something, but you might not have really internalized it. And you can internalize something, but not yet accepted it. So we're taking people through this process, or what they really want, and that's what the job of the landing page is, is to get them to accept that your view of the world as it relates to this topic that you're discussing that is of interest to your audience, that they are going to accept your worldview of it. They're going to be pulled into this world that you've created, because you've shown them the respect as the reader in this case, but you've shown them the respect to not, or the respect not just to believe that like, okay, I'll just tell you a few things and once I've told you those few things, you're just going to want to opt in. And we see this all the time, and this is why lead magnets and, you know, everybody keeps cranking up the dial on what they give away for free is because it's harder work to get somebody to know, believe, internalize and accept than it is to get somebody to want something bright and shiny. It's much easier just to say, I've got this free thing for you, just give me your email address, and I'll give you this really great free thing. I haven't accepted anything other than I want a free thing. I haven't internalized anything other than you're willing to give me stuff for my attention. That's all that does. And what we're trying to do is turn that around. And this is hard. And that's why most people don't do it. Most people don't sit down and say, okay, what actually has to, what does someone have to accept about my view of the world before they'll take that next step? And they really have to have to have to have to have internalized in the core of their being. That is really, really hard work. So the only way to do it is to sit down and actually do it. And some of you, and I would put myself in this category, I would love to intellectualize this all day long, but be so much fun and pull it apart and ask a million questions, and try to figure out like what's really going on here. But let us be very clear. What's really going on here is you need to get your ass in a chair and answer the questions that we just outline, and then write it. That's all that's really going on here because it's through the act of writing that everything in this, the entire workshop will make sense. And I suspect many of you are, we've seen the ads start coming in, and I think many of you are having that aha moment, or it's like, oh, now that I've sat down to do the work, the content makes a lot more sense. And in the landing pages now we're really cranking up the volume because it's you're going to, you know, at least double the amount of content to create, and a much higher hurdle at the end, you want somebody to opt in versus just click on an ad. So this, this requires sitting down and I would start by just making that list. What does somebody need to know, just get it out of your head. Well, it's boom 1234 they need to know these five things. Great. They might it might be what they need to know about the topic what they need to know about you, and your background, whatever it is, or what do they have to believe about that. To look at the list of things they need to know what from that what do they need to believe about those things. Now what do they have to start to internalize and accept like what are they like what are the fewer things that they need to accept and then it's practically written itself. Isn't that how it works Andre just writes itself. It feels like, of course, it's exactly what happens. In fact, I mean, we, we probably going to make ourselves look like clans now but actually, there we go. I've set the frame right there. So a few months ago, a few months ago in October, I've just brought up the email now. So it's October six. This was for our fall enrollment of our three courses and we interviewed the customer of ours, Jonathan Baker. And so that email is available on the site and you can we talk about it just just as a frame. And Jonathan is a is a is a professional or expert at training our performance athletes go faster with on their bicycles. And to answer that question, what does somebody need to believe? No, believe to want to go through the door. If this was Jonathan's page, for example, his call to action at this point, what he needs them to believe probably is this idea that power output. So he he talks about this concept that there's there's only three things that can make a buck that can make a bus go faster with the human on it. Arrow, weight and power output. But it's power output. That's that's that's the biggest lever. It's the thing where you can get the biggest gains. However, it's counterintuitive. Everybody wants to spend more money on on the best bicycle and, you know, remove as much aerodynamics as possible and weight. But it's the but those two things play the least. You know, they they contribute to the least in terms of of power. So that would be his question. That's how he would answer that. He he would want everybody that gets to the bottom of his page just before the opt-in form would be this idea that, oh, my God, it's power. It's it's this idea that my power output and I need to train power output. Obviously, Jonathan's the one that's that's going to show me how to train my power output efficiently. But that would be the whole thing that he would want them to get to by the time they get to the call to action. And at the same time, I think to slightly push back at the call to action, he could say something like, let me think that if if you still believe that that weight in a row is the thing that's going to contribute the most or whatever, then don't opt in because this isn't for you. So something like that. Obviously, at that point, people are people are thinking, hey, wait a minute, you know, I get this, I can understand this. And there'll be various levels of that. And those people will will go through that through that opt-in door, thinking very differently about what's about to happen next. There will be there will fundamentally be a different person than when they first experienced that landing page and and the Facebook ad that brought them to the landing page. So the hook at the top of that landing page would would lead into this this this whole idea. It's such a great example. Jonathan is such a great example because he's an expert in this field. I mean, he can really pull this off. But how he's done it, if you think about the power of that simple idea, if you're as ideal prospects, if you're a bicyclist, you want to go faster on the bicycle, like, not like a recreational, you know, maybe, you know, maybe you do a recreational, not professional, right? But these are serious cyclists. If you're a serious cyclist, you want to go faster on the bicycle and you bump into the guy who's been spending 30 years of his career as a PhD. And that's what he does. First of all, it's your lucky day, which changes that that's a frame change immediately. Jonathan's not looking. He's not lucky to have found you as a prospect. You're lucky to have found him as the expert. That's an immediate frame change. And that's not something he should. He wouldn't say that deliberately. But that's that's an implicit frame change. And it's a way to change our mindset to think about it. And then for somebody like Jonathan to say, listen, if you want X, which is, you know, he believes is their their core desire, their burning question, if you want to know how to go faster on the bicycle, there are only three ways to do it. Now, again, that's another that's another frame. He has essentially said, you might think they're 10,000 ways you can go faster, but I'm going to eliminate 9,997 of what you think might be possible and tell you there are only three. So that immediately elevates his level. He becomes an expert, but implicitly because he, he has looked at this and he knows, okay, there are only three things. And then he explains to you what those three things are and shows you clearly why two of them are not optimal. And within this, he's doing a frame within a frame, which is, you know, the larger frame is there three ways to go faster on a bicycle and here they are. But the frame within the frame is that there's a way to think about going faster on the bicycle that you probably haven't thought about before, which is this trade off between inputs and outputs. So you can spend a tremendous amount, it's like an efficiency curve almost. So you can spend a lot of money to shave off a little bit of weight, like by a really expensive bicycle to go just a tiny bit faster because that weight change doesn't make a big difference. Or you can spend lots of money to focus on aerodynamics and get a little bit of an output on that too. So now he's introduced an entirely different way to think about it, which is the way that he thinks about it. So the moment you agree to Jonathan's way of seeing the world, when he reveals the way he thinks about the best way to go faster on a bicycle is the trade off, input versus output, the highest ROI, the place you have the most leverage is by increasing your power output, learning how to be more powerful on the bicycle. The moment you nod your head yes to that, you have now stepped into a world that he has designed for you. He has designed the parameters. He has said, is you have now accepted his view of the world. You've said, I nodding up and down, like I want to go faster on a bicycle and that makes perfect sense. Once your prospect says, wow, that makes a lot of sense. And they take a step forward. They have stepped into your frame. And now you control the journey. And when I, to be clear, when we say all of these things, none of this is meant to be nefarious. It can be. All of the things we're talking about can and are abused. We're not talking about that. We're talking about using them in service to, using them legitimately in service to something that creates value. So for Jonathan, he is for the right people, he is able to show them how to go faster on a bicycle, but the way he's thought about it from a very long career focusing specifically on that topic. The moment you think like he does, or you nod your head, that makes a lot of sense, you've now stepped into his world and he is now the puppet master. Not manipulating people, but he has now defined for the entire journey, what's possible, what's not possible. And you've accepted it. Once you've nodded your head and said that makes sense. That's not to suggest that everybody's cheap. And once, if you say something stupid, when you're later, you try to sell something that doesn't make sense, that people are blindly zombies, like zombies, they'll do anything. That's not the point. But the point is you have now established the way your prospects are seeing the world that's about to unfold. And that is such a powerful, powerful lever. And it's also powerful for you as the writer, as the creator, because you don't, once they've stepped through that door, you don't have to go back and re-litigate everything. You don't have to keep going back and like, you know, are you sure you have to understand these things? They already understand them. They've already accepted them. Now you can continue the journey. In your mind, it becomes, okay, they've now accepted that this is how I see the world. Because of that, what should I, what do I need to do next? Like, how do we build a relationship? We'll talk about that next. But how do we build a relationship? How do I acknowledge them? I'm not just trying to sell them something, but I'm trying to teach first or to build that relationship first and knowing that selling will come later. That makes this whole process just incredibly, incredibly powerful. Yeah, and the other insight which we, which we eagerly ram down your throats whenever we get the opportunity is that at least in this example that we've just given you with Jonathan, 100% of people that land on that page aren't going to be the people that believe by the time they get to the bottom that power output is the thing that matters. And even if they do, they may not be the person that wants to put the effort in because that's hard. You know, training power output, which is your legs and your body, you know, they might get to that, get to the point and say, yeah, I believe everything you're saying, Jonathan, but that's not me. I don't want to do that. You know, then that's still a result for Jonathan because they haven't opted in and they haven't gone in. And, you know, so I think that's that's an important insight that we're not trying to capture everybody. We're only trying to capture those people that get it, that are enrolled in this journey that understand that, you know, certain things are required. And that's another part or requirement of the of the body copy. It's, it's, it's also painting the picture of that this is not all just unicorns and rainy and and and radius, right? There's not. It's not. No. I thought that's what we were selling. What you just said maybe reminded me this like, this is another, I love frames so much. I can't help I like everything's frames, but some of you just said, this is so counterintuitive. That's a great example. We, when we think about like conversion rates and overall performance, whatever it is, you know, opt in rate, but particularly customer conversion rate. We, we tend to think about that being impacted by the quality of the prospect that we attract, or if we think about it at all. And that's one way to frame it like, oh, if we could just get a higher quality prospect. Then our conversion rates would be better. But there's another way to think about it so we can change the frame. And we can frame that instead to say the way to improve your conversion rates to get more happier customers is not by the quality of the process. The way that you attract as much as it's by the quality of the prospect who you reject. So that's it's a very, it's a different way of looking, it sounds like it's the same thing. But what we tend to err on the side of we, we attract people into our world is like, hey, let's let everybody come in. And then there are going to be some margin, lots of marginal prospects, right? They're not really, they haven't really bought into our world. But if we just get enough of them, it'll make sense. And that's why, you know, you'll hear people brag about a 5% conversion rate, because it's, you know, it's three times the industry average. Like, wow, that's incredible. Which means 95% of the people who saw an offer didn't want it. Like, it's amazing to me that we've come to believe that 5% is great. The reason we think that is because we're enamored by this idea of the quality of the prospects we get. Instead of saying early in the process, let me just discourage everybody who's not going to be likely to convert later. And then you get the 20 and 30 and 40% conversion rates. Not because of who you attracted as much as by because of the people who you specifically rejected or discouraged in the beginning. It's such a weird way to think about it. But to me, it's powerful. Like, instead of trying so hard to say, how can we get the ideal prospect to go through the next step? That's too hard. But what I can do is figure out how do I put up roadblocks for people who I don't want to come through? Because it's a lot easier to make a list of one of the characteristics of people who are going to be a pain later. You know, like what, and it's, you know, someone who maybe is arguing with the main thesis that I've made or someone who just doesn't really buy it. And is, you know, kind of on the fence and they're not sure and they want me to convince them that I'm right. I don't want to do that. Like, I just rather early on to say, just go away. Not disrespectfully, but just go away. Because what I want are the people who finally make it through to be I want to have vetted them so well that they're just so much more likely to buy. I don't know why that just popped into my head when we were talking about that. Yeah, that's we can talk forever. So we have to be careful here. So I have I have an idea for the last thing. I'm going to say my last, what I think is the last most important thing I want to contribute to today's conversation. And then I think you might riff on it. But if not, then you you riff on your last last thing is we don't force ourselves to stop this. We're going to do five hour calls every week. Because we love talking about this stuff. Okay, so here's here's what I think is. Here's what I want to draw everyone's attention to. And the thinking about this module. This is listed in the content under the pro tip. This idea that I'll just read it quickly. It's only two paragraphs. So here's the pro tip we included in the content. The organizing question you're answering with a landing page should relate directly to the most important promise you make in your offer. Remember, you know where this journey leads, but the prospect does not. We want the landing page to attract prospects who are ideal candidates for the offer will make later in the customer's journey. We're speaking to an amplify in that desire now, but we're not completely resolving that underlying tension for several days until prospects can finally buy something. The reason I want to draw attention to this is that it's, it's easy to to forget that we're building a system and to focus on the linear nature of the steps of the system. And so, okay, I've got this ad and I said a frame that I've got this overall thematic arc I set the frame, I brought people through an ad and now I have no landing page I'm going to get them to opt in, and you can do some great word craft here and you can like lots of things can you can get a lead, but you don't, you don't just want the lead, you want the lead who's going to become a customer. So the landing page, and the ad, like all of it, the implicit promises that you're making, or explicit, but the thing that you're talking about, like in the case where we mentioned Jonathan's offer eventually is training people to go faster on the bicycle by increasing their power output. So it makes perfect sense that when he gets all the way back to the beginning of the journey with the ad and the landing page, that he needs his ideal prospects to step through the door of belief to have internalized that going faster on a bicycle the best way to do that is to increase power output. Because by the time he actually makes something available that they can buy to accomplish something it's 100% can grow it. I realize this sounds like a blinding flash of the obvious but I've seen far too many times I've seen landing pages that get somebody to nod their head yes to something that isn't actually the thing that's delivered in the what's for sale later, it might be kind of like the thing, but it's not exactly like it, and that little bit of mismatch will throw everything off. Because again we're reverse engineering from the final outcome that we want which we want an incredibly happy superfan if we that's the ideal. The person who says this is exactly the thing that I've been looking for I am so thankful that I found you, I'm so excited to get started. And we're taking, we're imagining that person and imagine the entire journey that it takes to get them to that point to be desperate to want to buy the thing that you have to offer. And because of that the entire we want to, even though we're introducing all these micro frictions along the way to remove the wrong prospects, we do still have within all of those friction points. I'm imagining it's almost like a water slide like we want the right candidate to slide from beginning to end and have like a pop out of the end of the water slide with credit card and and thinking this is so I'm so excited. This is exactly what I've been looking for. So, all right, I'm not going to say anymore today other than I don't believe it. Thank you. Your turn. I don't know if you want to riff on that or if you want to riff on something entirely different. Yeah, I think it's good that you've that you've drawn attention to to the obvious. It's obvious for us, but it's it's certainly not obvious for everybody. I don't think because it's very easy to fall into the trap that a landing page is a self contained thing just like a Facebook ad is a self contained thing. It's so easy because that's what that's how people have been drumming on for since since forever. But as you said, this is part of a system and the system that we're optimizing for is this macro level thing that we want this, this happy customer. And we know what that looks like at the very end. So when we when we wrote the little customer POV story in in the lesson for this week of Joe and Joe is this lady that's that's experiencing joint pain. That's not arthritis. So yes, it was fictional. Actually, it's actually based on a real story. So Joe is an actual that's not a real name, but she's based on a real person that was featured in it in a documentary. So we haven't completely pulled this out of our arse. That story or that we have fictionalized it for the for the for the point of this exercise and to demonstrate how you guys should be writing these customers journeys. But, you know, Joe is the is the ultimate happy customer at the end. That's that's Joe in the context of the joint pain example. But she's now interacting with the sales page, which is still fairly early in in the big system, you know, in the process. So when the landing page is rigged up in a way that's congruent and it speaks a language that she completely resonates with and vibrates with excitement by the time she gets to the call to action, which is kind of what we how we tried to to write that story. That's that's the customer just interacting with a piece of the system that's not the whole system. And it's not near the end of the system yet. It's just this thing somewhere in the middle. And that's that's that's that that's the frame that you got to keep thinking of will keep coming back to that at every single, you know, the system is made up of parts and all these parts interact with each other to create this emergent effect, which is the which is Joe finding this thing and wanting to buy this product and it's going to change your life and everything. So when when she's interacting with this page, she's she's having these emotional feelings because of how their page is is written and and and the certain things that we want Joe to believe if we were selling cure in this space. Yeah, so I think that's what I wanted to say is do the exercise on writing your version of Joe or whatever the the the mail. I forget what we wrote on the page. Yeah, that was our buddy Raymond. He's a good guy. Raymond. Yeah. And it's funny, like, we have to do this deliberately at first. You have to add it sounds ridiculous. And I think I suspect a lot of our customers are going to students are going to be thinking to themselves this is nonsense. I'm not going to do this. Right. And let's be honest, but the point is we just to be clear, we don't sit down and write narratives about Ray and Joe every time that we write something. But that's because our equivalent of Ray and Joe live inside our heads all the time. So every week we've made a habit. We don't know how to not do this because we have forced ourselves to do it so many times so that every time whenever we're writing something or contemplating writing something or creating something, Ray and Joe are in our heads already guiding us. You know, the question I ask myself all the time I've written something is, why does she care? Why does he care? Why would anybody and we have relationships with many of our clients or customers and we I think about many of them as individuals like we know their businesses in many ways. So I will think about like in this workshop, there are a lot of people in this workshop who I consider to be my friends. And I'm thinking about them as we're creating this content. That's an example of what we're talking about here. You're asking all the time you're thinking you're thinking about what you want to create. And then you're asking yourself as a check in between. Here's what I think. But then you're taking that perspective that POV shift and looking like, okay, well, what does she want? Like how does this relate to what she wants? And what happens on the other side of that little interaction is so much more powerful. And the only way to learn it is to do it. I said I wasn't going to say anymore. Alright, let's wrap up at this point where it's going to be seven hours. This is going to be the five week workshop that has 17 hours worth of audio content. You can't shut up about this stuff. Alright, everybody, thank you very much for your attention. Listen to this extra part of week three. Let us know in the comments. And we have thick skins. If this doesn't work for any reason, if you don't feel like it's contributing, let us know that. If it does work or if we could make it better, just leave a comment. Send it if you prefer to send an email. Send us an email. Just let us know. Alright, so what we're going to do is this audio, this extra that we've included, does that add enough value for the time that you're exchanging for it? We are going to plan on, we're planning on doing this for the remainder. But it certainly will be listening to your feedback as well. Do your homework for this this week. It is due by midnight Pacific time. If you want us to review it, it will not be easy. Do not expect it to be easy. We keep saying over and over again, all of the success that you're looking for is on the other side of the work. So do the damn work. Thanks everybody. Take care.
